speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] have to be in front. Put it against you. There we go. Go. This is Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. Today is August 18, 1995. We are interviewing Hyman Skulsky. The interviewer is Norm Pell. We're in the Pacific Palisades in California, United States of America. And the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Norman Pell. Today is August 18, 1995. For the Survivors of the Shoah Foundation, we are in Pacific Palisades, California in the United States of America to interview Mr. Hyman Skulsky. The interview will be conducted in English. Good afternoon, Mr. Skulsky. Would you please give us your full name, and let us know when and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Hyman Skulsky. And I was born in London, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on May 5th, 1915.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us a little about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents came from Russia. They ran away in 1905 from Russia to Belgium. And in 1914, when the World War number one started, they ran away as refugees to England. And I was born in England-- in London-- in consequence of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us, what were your parents' names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Lazar, or Elazar, and my mother's name was Rosa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was the same as my father's name, Skulsky, because both my grandfathers were brothers. And my mother and my father were first cousins. Very unusual to get a maiden name of my mother and father the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us a little about the rest of your family. Brothers? Sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one older brother by the name of Abraham. And he was born in 1909. And he was a music critic, and he passed away in 1959 as a consequence of a heart attack in Venice, Italy. And he's buried there in the Jewish cemetery in Venice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any other brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, I have another older brother by the name of Samuel. He lives in Beverly Hills. And thank God he is OK, except for his eyesight. He's two years older than I am. He was born in Belgium in 1913. And I had one younger sister by the name of Sophie, and she was killed in the Holocaust. She was sent to Birkenau and never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little about your childhood. Do you remember much of what it was like in London at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left London at the age of five. And I remember a couple of very small incidents. I must have been about two or three years old when my mother woke me up, took me in her arms, and took me down to the basement, to the cellar, because a zeppelin was coming to-- was bombing the town. And then, I remember one other incident, that was Armistice 1918, when we were taken to the park. And I remember the fireworks, which was, for me, the first time I saw fireworks. And London was all lit up for the first time in my life, because during the war, London was blacked out-- or the whole of England was blacked out. And it was a terrific experience to see fireworks. And shortly afterwards, maybe a year or two afterwards, I had polio. And I was very-- I couldn't walk. And the doctor promised my mother that he would operate on me and it wouldn't be worse. But there was a chance that it might improve my leg. And so he did. And I spent almost a year in the hospital, London's Children's Hospital. And thank God, when I came out, I lived all my life a normal life, except that I limp a little bit. But I never had any problems from this leg, and I can walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you were five, you moved.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was another thing. My father had to-- didn't want to go into the army. Like many Jewish people who came from Russia, the army was the end of the line. This was the worst. So he and a whole group of other refugees went back to Russia without my mother and the children. They said the war would be over in a year or two. And they would not have to serve in an army. And they would come back. So it was. But while I had this polio, my mother was a refugee. My mother had to go back to Belgium where she had come from. But they made the exception for me. I could stay in the hospital until I was well. So I was left alone there. My mother went back with my-- with her other two children to Belgium-- to Antwerp. And about six-- five or six months later, my mother's brother, my uncle, came from Belgium and took me back to Belgium, where I joined the family. And about two or three months later, my father returned from Russia. So it was a grand reunion of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this reunion was in Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Belgium, in Antwerp. My father was-- was in the diamond business. He learned to cut diamonds before the war. And he was an expert in rough diamonds. So he started to go into the diamond business and gradually worked himself into it again. And he made a nice living.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your childhood in Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to cheder. I went to a Jewish school called Tachkemoni. It's still there. The school is still there. And then when I got a little older, I think I was about 11-- no, I was 12 years old when my parents put me into a-- what did we call it? The city school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Public school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school, yes. And I finished the public school. And then I went into a trade school-- electrician trade school-- in Antwerp. And when I came out, my brother had also finished his trade school to become electrical engineer. We both went into business together. My brother Samuel and I opened a little shop. And although my father wanted us to join him in the diamond business, I don't know why, but I was not interested in diamonds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a little. And tell us a little about what Antwerp was like. What kind of a Jewish population did it have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Antwerp had the population of about 32,000 Jews. And it grew later before World War number two started. Many German refugees escaped to Belgium. And there were about 10,000 refugees in Antwerp. And, in fact, I had many a friend who was a refugee. But we had a very nice Jewish social life there. It was everything there-- [INAUDIBLE] and Maccabi and Zionists and everything in a small area. And there wasn't a week we did not attend one of these gatherings. And I personally belonged to a Jewish club for youngsters called Machaneh Yisrael. And most of them were Dutch Jewish people between, I would say, about 18 and 25 years old. And we used to go every Tuesday night. We used to meet in the-- in a little hall or in a coffee shop. And we used to invite a doctor or a lawyer or another public speaker to give a lecture. And then, there was a question and answer group. And then we went out dancing sometimes. We were a very close, nice, warm club.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very. They were very Orthodox, Modern Orthodox. They were not Hasidic but very Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Celebrated all the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every holiday. They celebrated every holiday. So did we children, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about your standard of living as a family. How would you characterize it? Were you well off?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had everything we wanted. We didn't lack anything. In fact, my father was very-- I don't know the word. For instance, I wanted to buy a car. And in those days, in the '30s, a car was a luxury. But I needed one for my business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took six lessons in one week-- nobody knew about it-- secretly. And when I came home and I told my parents, I said, you know-- I even had the certificate that I finished my class. They said, you're mad. You can get killed. And another thing, if you get the car, everybody will think we got rich. And this was the mentality those days. How did the Jewish population get along with the non-Jewish population? Were there any problems in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, very little problems, no. There were anti-Semites like in every country, like here. But generally, the general Jewish population got along very well with the non-Jewish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family take vacations? Tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We, in the early-- even in the late '20s, we used to go-- my mother used to go every year to the country for six weeks, a place called [INAUDIBLE]. It's real country there with-- I still remember when we used to go there. We used to go every evening, walk with a milk can to the farmer, and he used to milk. We used to stand while the farmer was milking the cows. And we used to buy the fresh milk and take it home in the cans. And it was lovely, a real farm life. And then later in the '30s, we used to go to the beach, to Ostend, which is a harbor-- a Belgium harbor town. And we used to rent an apartment for six weeks every summer. And in fact, the last summer before the World War in 1939, we had a gathering of family from everywhere. Even a cousin from Norway came. And he joined us there on the beach and, naturally, went home. And it was a terrific gathering of family, the last one before most of them were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before the war, did you do any work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had the-- we had the lighting business as well, a lamp store. And we also went into parts for bicycles, wholesale business. We used to travel-- I used to travel around selling parts to bicycle stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in that business with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: About five years-- four or five years, until the war started in 1940. And when my brother Sam, with whom I was in partnership, he had married already. And when the Germans came, he said he doesn't want to stay while the Germans were there. And he ran away to unoccupied France. And then a few months later, he had his wife sent there as well with a child. And I was still in Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now was there any indication of problems in Belgium that prompted your brother to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were in Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came. They occupied Belgium in May 1940. And I-- I'm a British subject by birth. And I had a British passport. And it so happened that in April of 1940, I went to England on business. And I came back to Antwerp on May 9, a Thursday evening. I've come back. I had a lovely trip over the Channel on the channel boat, which takes about 3 and 1/2 hours. When I came home-- I still lived at home, by the way-- my parents were glad to see me. We had dinner. And I gave them regards from the family in London. And then, at 2:00 in the morning, suddenly we heard shots, shooting, guns, and we woke up. We all got up. And we turned the radio on. And we were told that the Germans had just started to invade Belgium. That was on May 10, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next morning on May 10, the first thing was there was a run on the banks. Everybody wanted-- they needed the money. So I remember I also went. We stood in line, and we got the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people said they were leaving town and going towards the coast, because at the coast they had two chances. They could either try and get on a boat to England or they could cross the French border and go into France. This was the first day of the war, mind you. My father said, it's Friday today. Tonight is Shabbat. And Shabbat, we forget the-- we forget the war. And on Saturday night, we'll decide what we're going to do. So it was a very nervous Shabbat. We went to shul, to the synagogue. And there were very few people there. And those that were there, everybody was talking about leaving town. And Saturday night, we decided on Sunday morning we're going to leave as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we spent the whole night packing as much as we could carry. And on Sunday morning, we went to the station with the [INAUDIBLE], with the-- and we left town for-- on the train that was going to Ostend. Halfway from there is a town called Ghent. And we were told there that the trains will not continue to Ostend today. We all had to get off in Ghent. So there were thousands of refugees there in this town. And the-- I don't know who it was-- some officials took us to a school in Ghent. We spent the night there. And the next morning, we got on another train and we reached Ostend. You remember the family going through the packing to leave? What was that like, deciding what to take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was very upset and nervous and excited. We didn't know what tomorrow will bring. We didn't think the Germans would occupy the whole country. This was-- you know we had the-- Belgium had the defense line, the Albert Canal. They thought no Germans would be able to cross it. But they made a mistake. It took them two hours, I believe, and they were on the other side. But anyway, when we got to Ostend, we got-- by tramway, we got to a town called Koksijde, which was near De Panne, near the French border. And we didn't-- there were thousands of people. Normally, the city of Koksijde, which is a seaside resort, holds only about maybe 700 people-- inhabitants. And there were half a million people there in the area of Koksijde and De Panne, two little towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had an old customer there, whom I knew. So I went to him, and I told him, I said, maybe you can let us have a room or something. He said, I've got a villa on the front-- on the front of the sea. I'll rent it to you. I said how much do you want? He want a thousand francs, which is nothing, $20 for the month.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I'll take it. And we went there. And it was a beautiful three-story building overlooking the sea-- the North Sea. And we moved in there. A couple of other cousins from Antwerp-- one couple was my father's partner in the business-- he went there with his wife. And my sister was there. And we have a lovely house. And then, as days moved on, the war came there. The British were-- the Dutch had given up. The Belgians were about to give up. And the British airplanes at the Royal Air Force were fighting with the German planes, with the [INAUDIBLE]. And we were standing in the window and just looking out through the window and watching the war. And in fact, I used to go to the beach and lie down on the beach and look up and see the war going on there. Then a week later, the British had decided they're going to evacuate the whole army from Dunkirk, which was only about 10 miles away from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the whole British army that was on the continent came onto the beaches with all their trucks, with all their supplies, food, guns, anything. And they shot the tires of the trucks, left them there fully supplied, except for the food, and they started-- hundreds of boats came from England to pick them up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was on the beach with them. I tried to get away. I was a civilian. They were in the army. So they wouldn't take me. Until one day, they-- we decided we're going to try and cross into France. So we walked to the border, which was about five miles away or six miles away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we came to the border-- the French border, [INAUDIBLE] or whatever-- wouldn't let my parents cross the border because they had Nansen [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] passes. What do you call them? Visas or whatever. They couldn't-- passports. So they were not allowed to cross into France.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could cross anytime I wanted with a British passport. But I couldn't leave them there on their own. So I went back with my parents back to De Panne. The house we had rented for a month had been, in the meantime, occupied by other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we found another little home and a house. And we stayed there for another two weeks, I believe. And that was much worse. The war had progressed. The British were leaving. The German guns were bombing the city of De Panne and Dunkirk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dunkirk was ablaze. You could see the black smoke coming out. And then one Friday night, the bombing got much worse. And we all went down to the air raid shelter and spent the whole night down in the cellar. In the morning it got-- suddenly it was quiet. So I said I'm going upstairs to see what's going on. My mother said be careful, be careful. Anyway, I went up to the second floor and looked out through the window, and I saw the first German soldiers. They had occupied the town of De Panne and Dunkirk. And they started shouting all the refugees have to go home where they came from. So we were practically chased out of there. And off we went to the next town. We walked. It was 10 kilometers from there. It was called Veurne, a town 10 kilometers away from De Panne. We came to Veurne. We spent a night there in the cellar. And this time, the British Royal Air Force was bombing the German-- the Germans who had occupied the town. Anyway, we spent the night there. Next day, on we went. On the way home, we were refugees. I was pushing a bike with luggage on top of it and the others were following. And there were thousands of people walking with packages. Anyway, the next night we spent at the farms house, a very nice farmer. And he said you can sleep in the barn with the chickens. So we slept there. And the next morning, he served us a lovely breakfast. And he charged us next to nothing. He was very nice. He was a nice man. Then the following day, we came--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now just bring us up to date. Who was with you now? It's you--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my mother, my sister, two cousins, and I. My brother had left on his own with his wife. He went. And the other brother hadn't left at all. He lived in Brussels. We found out later. We had no idea what happened to him. Anyway, we got to another town. I don't remember the name of it. And the Germans had trucks available to take everyone to their hometown. There was a truck for Antwerp. So we were loaded onto the truck. They were very nice at that time. And they took us back home. And when we came home, it was a pleasure to be home again after a month being on the road.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were gone for a total of one month.

speaker: interviewee
text: One month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the house was still intact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was intact there, yeah, at that time. This was in June 1940, we were home again. The quietness didn't last long. Exactly a week later, one morning around 7:00, the bell goes on the house, and a Belgian policeman comes in. He says, I want to speak to Hyman Skulsky. So I was there. I said, that's me. I'm sorry, I've got to take you along. I said, what for? He doesn't know. I've got to come with him. I'm arrested. I said, OK. So I went. And my mother was crying. My father was crying. And he took us to a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a police station. This was a police station the first time. And they locked us in a room. And there was a whole group of all British subjects, only British civilians. And we spent a day there. And then in the evening, a bus came along and took us to Brussels, which is only half an hour away. And they dropped us off in an exhibition hall, a very nice exhibition hall called the Cinquantenaire in Brussels. And we had banks, lovely banks. And there was, like, a balcony in front with big steps. We could sit there. But German guards were patrolling. We were not allowed to leave. And we got food there. And we thought, this is lovely. To be a prisoner this way is a pleasure. And we spent three days there. And on the fourth day, it was Friday morning, suddenly we hear shouting. But you know German shouting. [INAUDIBLE] You know, I can't imitate them. It was so rough and so like canon balls. And this was at 6:00 in the morning. And we had to fall in-- in line, stand to attention, and we stood there from 6:00 in the morning till around 11:00, 11:30. And all the time, there were three or four SS soldiers who were telling us they're going to shoot us, they're going to take us to Germany, and they're going to kill us, and they're going to bury us. You know, the worst that you-- and we believed them. And we were standing for all these hours without moving. Then around 11:30, a truck came along-- a German army truck-- or two, I don't remember. And they loaded us. And they took us. We were going east. I know Belgium very well-- every village, every town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're going, and I thought we were going to Germany. Thank God they did not take us to Germany. But they stopped in a big army camp, which is called Leopoldsberg, about maybe 50 miles or 40 miles. I don't know the-- how far exactly. And they dropped the-- We'll continue in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.
